question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers:
This systematic review was based on a registered (PROSPERO CRD42012002552) and published protocol (Hansen et al., 2012).

Our main objective was to evaluate the effects of BASs versus placebo or no interventions on glycemic control and adverse events in type 2 diabetes. We originally planned to compare BAS versus other antidiabetic interventions, but only identified one trial comparing BASs versus rosiglitazone or sitagliptin (Rigby et al., 2010). We decided not to include data from the rosiglitazone arm in our analyses because the drug has been associated with increased risk of myocardial infarction but not cardiovascular or all cause mortality (Nissen & Wolski, 2010). Our primary endpoint was glycemic control (HbA1c). Secondary endpoints included adverse events and changes in fasting plasma glucose, total cholesterol, high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, LDL cholesterol, triglycerides and body weight.

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers: Aim To evaluate the effects of bile acid sequestrants (BASs) versus placebo, no intervention or active comparators on glycemic control in type 2 diabetes.

question: Were eligibility criteria unambiguous?
answers:
We included trials of adult patients of both genders with type 2 diabetes as defined by the authors of the included trials. The BAS groups included cholestyramine, colesevelam colestimide or colestipol, irrespective of daily dose or duration of the trial.

Protocol - PICOs

question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria
answers: We included randomized controlled trials, irrespective of date, publication status, language, bias control and predefined outcome.

question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers:
We included randomized controlled trials, irrespective of date, publication status, language, bias control and predefined outcome.

We also searched the WHO International Clinical Trial Registry Platform and conference proceedings from the annual scientific meetings of the American Diabetes Association (ADA) and the European Association for the Study of Diabetes (EASD).

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers: Eligible trials were identified by electronic searches in The Cochrane Library, Medline, Embase, the Web of Science and clinicaltrials.gov

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers: The reference lists of relevant articles were manually examined and relevant trials identified. Additional information on trials was sought through correspondence with authors and pharmaceutical companies.

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers: The search terms in the Cochrane Library were ("bile acid sequestrant" or "cholestyramine" or "colestimide" or "colestipol" or "colesevelam") and "type 2 diabetes". The search strategy was adjusted to fit the other databases and combined with the Cochrane Search String for the identification of randomized clinical trials ("Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions").

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers: We included randomized controlled trials, irrespective of date, publication status, language, bias control and predefined outcome.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers:
2.2 Study Selection - Two authors independently reviewed the trial inclusion and exclusion criteria.

Protocol - Selection of studies: Trials identified through the electronic and manual searches will be listed and included trials will be selected using the criteria described above. Excluded trials will be listed with the reason for exclusion. All authors will participate in the selection or trials for inclusion.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers:
Protocol: Data collection and analysis: Two authors (MH and DPS) will independently extract data and resolve disagreements through discussion before  analysis. In case of unresolved matters, a third party will be involved.

Article: Data extraction was performed independently by three authors based on standardized extraction forms. Disagreements were resolved through discussion.

question: Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
answers:
The risk of bias was assessed by separate domains: random sequence generation, allocation concealment, blinding of participants, personnel and outcome assessment, incomplete outcome data, selective reporting and other bias ("Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions")

We retrieved unpublished information about trial design and outcome measures from 16 trials

Table 3. Risk of bias assessment

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
answers: nan

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers: Table 1. Characteristics of included trials

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers:
Random-effects metaanalysis showed that patients randomized to BASs had a lower hemoglobin A1c at the end of treatment compared with the control group (mean difference -0.55%; 95% confidence interval -0.64 to -0.46). Analysis of trials with low risk of bias in all domains confirmed the findings.

ONLINE SUPPLEMENTARY: Fig 3. Mean change in body weight random-effects meta-analysis

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers: The meta-analyses were performed using random-effects based on expected clinical heterogeneity. We repeated the analyses using fixed-effect models. In all analyses, the overall conclusion of the two models concurred, but random-effects models provided the most conservative estimate of the intervention effect. We therefore only report the result of the random-effects meta-analyses.

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
We used I2 values as markers of heterogeneity

sensitivity analyses were performed to evaluate heterogeneity

the heterogeneity between the trials with a placebo control group was considerable (I2=90.6). Sensitivity analyses on trials with a low risk of bias assessed using individual domains, confirmed the findings (MD -0.44%; -0.51 to -0.37; I2=91.6%; 9 trials).

sub-group analyses showed a significant difference (P<0.01) between trials on colesevelam (MD -0.59 mmol/L; -0.60 to -0.57) and colestimide -1.30 mmol/L; -1.64 to - 0.96).

Protocol: Subgroup analysis and investigation of heterogeneity: Subgroup analyses will be performed to analyse the influence of patient, intervention and trial characteristics intertrial heterogeneity. The subgroup analyses will compare the different types of BASs. The test for subgroup differences will be calculated and the results presented as P and I-squared values.

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
answers:
Funnel plots and regression tests for funnel plot asymmetry in metaanalysis were performed for meta-analyses with at least ten trials to assess small study effects (Egger test for continuous and Harbord for dichotomous outcomes). The effects of interventions were examined using subgroup and sensitivity analyses.

no evidence of publication bias or small study effects.

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
We evaluated bias control and subgroup and sensitivity analyses were performed to evaluate heterogeneity and bias.

Analysis of trials with low risk of bias in all domains confirmed the findings. 

Data on adverse events were limited. There were no differences between trials stratified by the control group and no evidence of publication bias or small study effects.

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers: We included randomized controlled trials, irrespective of date

answers: Aim To evaluate the effects of bile acid sequestrants (BASs) versus placebo, no intervention or active comparators on glycemic control in type 2 diabetes.

question: Did the reviewers avoid emphasizing results on the basis of their statistical significance?
answers: Yes
